hook.text: The ocean absorbs a quarter of all CO₂ we emit. The catch: it turns the water acidic.

buildup.text: CO₂ dissolves in seawater and reacts to form carbonic acid. This lowers the ocean's pH.

discovery.text: Since 1800, ocean acidity has increased 30%. That's faster than any time in 300 million years.

twist.text: Acid dissolves calcium carbonate—the material corals and shellfish use to build their shells.

climax.text: If the trend continues, coral reefs could dissolve by 2100. A chemical reaction threatening entire ecosystems.

punchline.text: CO₂ plus seawater equals acid. The ocean's chemistry is changing.

quiz.question: Why does ocean acidification threaten coral reefs?
options: Acid dissolves the calcium carbonate that corals use for their skeletons
Acid makes the water too hot for corals
Acid blocks sunlight from reaching corals
